heading: SAGGIO INTRODUTTIVO XCV
card_summary: This chunk of Campanini's introductory essay presents and analyzes Zorzi's bibliographic catalogue of Kabbalistic sources (the "Hebraeorum scriptorum ingens et numerosus catalogus") as it appeared in the 1546 Paris edition of De Harmonia Mundi. Campanini reproduces the full list — ranging from Enoch and Abraham (Sefer Yetzirah) to Simeon ben Yochay (Zohar), Abraham Abulafia, Yosef Gikatilla, Menahem Recanati, and dozens of anonymous treatises — and then provides item-by-item identification of each source. Key discussions include: the attribution debate over the Sefer Yetzirah (Abraham vs. Rabbi Aqiva); Zorzi's probable dependence on Reuchlin's De arte cabalistica for his knowledge of the Zohar; the identification of "Elcana" with the Sefer ha-Peli'ah, whose manuscript provenance connects to Zorzi's library at S. Francesco della Vigna; and the hermeneutic significance of Zorzi's interpretation of Genesis "lek leka" as "Vade ad te," which closely parallels Zoharic exegesis.
essay:
# Zorzi's Kabbalistic Source Catalogue: Campanini's Analytical Commentary (pp. 95–99)

## Context and Scholarly Reception

Campanini opens this section by situating Zorzi's Kabbalistic bibliography within a history of scholarly neglect. He notes that previous scholars, including the author of an important study on Zohar reception among Christian Kabbalists, prudently refrained from commenting on the catalogue. William Melczer is quoted as emblematic of the frustrated scholarly consensus:

> "Nel De Harmonia mundi viene citato un gran numero di testi cabbalistici; e nessuno ha ancora analizzato a fondo quell'opera così ricca di richiami esoterico-ermetici con un occhio analitico volto verso la cabala"

<cite>p. 95, fn. 410, quoting Melczer</cite>

Campanini frames his own contribution as the first sustained attempt at analytical identification of Zorzi's Kabbalistic sources, drawing a parallel to his earlier work identifying sources in Reuchlin's *De arte cabalistica*.

## The Catalogue: Structure and Spirit

The catalogue first appeared in the *Index maxime necessarius* in the 1546 Paris edition of *De Harmonia Mundi*. Zorzi's own framing is significant: he presents the works listed as those he personally consulted, and explicitly excludes others he had not yet been able to access — signaling both genuine familiarity and the collector's desire for more. The heading in the Index is **"Hebraeorum scriptorum ingens et numerosus catalogus."**

The catalogue itself spans a wide range: it begins with antediluvian and patriarchal authorities (Enoch, Abraham), moves through rabbinic giants (Simeon ben Yochay, Hai Gaon, Abraham ibn Ezra), named medieval Kabbalists (Abraham Abulafia, Yosef Gikatilla, Menahem Recanati, Nahmanides/Ramban, Rabbi Yosef Albo, Moses de León, Rabbi Bahiyah), commentators on the Sefer Yetzirah, and closes with a long list of anonymous treatises whose titles Campanini identifies where possible: *Sha'ar ha-Or* (Porta della luce), *Sha'ar ha-Tzedek* (Porta di giustizia), *Sefer ha-Razim*, *Sefer ha-Sod*, *Sefer ha-Temunot*, *Merkavah di Ezechiele*, *Imre Shefer*, and others.

Campanini observes that the catalogue's rhetorical framing — opening with remotest antiquity and closing with unnamed desiderata — fits the "spirito del tempo":

> "Come si vede, il catalogus si apre e si chiude, in linea con lo spirito del tempo, alludendo a confini quanto mai imprecisati, tra opere di antichità remotissima e volumi adespoti, o desiderata cui non si rinuncia, nella speranza, prima o poi, di venirne in possesso, come aveva detto Giovanni Pico della Mirandola, pronti a sborsare qualunque somma"

<cite>p. 95–96</cite>

This situates Zorzi within the broader Christian Kabbalist economy of manuscript desire, in which texts circulated rarely and Jews were reluctant to sell or copy them.

## Item-by-Item Identification

### 1. Enoch (Hanoc)

Zorzi cites Enoch as the first Kabbalist, referencing the Epistle of Jude and Origen's *Peri Archon*. Campanini notes that at least three Enochic texts exist (1 Enoch/Ethiopic, 2 Enoch/Slavonic, 3 Enoch/Hebrew Hekhalot text), and that Zorzi's patristic citations point toward 1 Enoch, but his mention elsewhere of *Merkavah Ezechielis* (a Hekhalot text) makes it plausible that Zorzi also knew or had indirect knowledge of 3 Enoch.

### 2. Abraham — Sefer Yetzirah (*Liber de formatione*)

Campanini identifies this without hesitation as the *Sefer Yetzirah*, noting that Zorzi cited it extensively throughout *De Harmonia Mundi*. Zorzi includes the traditional attribution to Abraham but also records the view that Rabbi Aqiva was the actual compiler — a note already present in Reuchlin's *De arte cabalistica*, from which Zorzi likely derived it. Campanini highlights Zorzi's sophisticated understanding of Jewish pseudepigraphy: even if Aqiva compiled the text, Zorzi argues it belongs to an "intemporale" deposit of revealed knowledge, a chain of transmission (*qabbalah* as oral reception) whose coherence is guaranteed by the *philosophia perennis* that *De Harmonia Mundi* itself is designed to map.

### 3. Simeon ben Yochay — Zohar (*Zoar*)

The Zohar receives extended discussion. Campanini is careful to distinguish between Zorzi's *mention* of the Zohar and his *actual use* of it in *De Harmonia Mundi*. The evidence for genuine direct consultation is ambiguous: most references to Simeon ben Yochay in *De Harmonia Mundi* do not imply Zoharic provenance, and Zorzi's account of the Zohar (that Simeon spent twenty-four years in a cave composing it) may derive entirely from Reuchlin. However, two passages give pause. First, Zorzi writes:

> "De quorum sacrificiorum sacramentis Rabi Simeon Ben Iochai super Leviticum, celeberrimum et amplissimum volumen aedidit"

<cite>p. 98, fn. 423 (Latin from De Harmonia Mundi)</cite>

This clearly refers to the Zohar's commentary on Leviticus. More striking is Zorzi's exegesis of Genesis 12:1 (*lek leka*): dissatisfied with the Vulgate rendering *Exi de terra tua*, he reads the Hebrew directly and arrives at the interpretation **"Vade ad te"** — "go to yourself," an inward journey. Campanini notes this closely parallels the Zohar's own reading of the same verse, though he cannot exclude independent derivation or a secondary source.

### 4. Elcana — Sefer ha-Peli'ah

This is the most securely identified item in the catalogue. Campanini identifies "Elcana" with the *Sefer ha-Peli'ah*, a late medieval Kabbalistic work. Two factors confirm it: Zorzi explicitly cites this source in *De Harmonia Mundi*, and a manuscript copy of the *Sefer ha-Peli'ah* preserved at the Bodleian Library (MS dated 1565, copied by Vittorio Eliano, nephew of Elia Levita, for the English knight Thomas Markenfield) bears a frontispiece note connecting it to Zorzi's library at the convent of S. Francesco della Vigna in Venice — the only extant indirect material trace of Zorzi's Kabbalistic holdings.

## Larger Significance for De Harmonia Mundi

This section is pivotal for understanding the epistemological architecture of *De Harmonia Mundi*. Zorzi's catalogue is not a mere bibliographic display but a claim about the *philosophia perennis*: the Kabbalists are listed alongside classical and patristic authorities because, for Zorzi, all genuine wisdom traditions transmit the same primordial revelation. The *qabbalah* is by definition "oral reception" (*qabbalah* = ricezione orale), which authenticates its antiquity. The structure of the catalogue — from Enoch and Abraham through medieval Sephardic and Italian Kabbalists to anonymous treatises — enacts this vision of unbroken transmission. Campanini's painstaking source-identification reveals how variable Zorzi's actual access to these texts was, ranging from texts he clearly read (Sefer Yetzirah, Sefer ha-Peli'ah) to those he knew primarily through Reuchlin's mediation (possibly the Zohar) to names and titles he collected in the hope of eventual acquisition.
